takeaways: Landmark Properties pivots to senior housing as 86% of investors plan to increase sector exposure — analysts say the 30–50% discounts off 2019 pricing peaks are about to disappear, making now a critical acquisition window.

CRE capital markets are thawing: commercial mortgage rates sit at 5.39% with the Fed holding at 3.50–3.75%, and Colliers projects a 15–20% jump in transaction volume across 2026 as institutional capital returns.

Proptech VC surged 68% to $16.7B in 2025 as agentic AI moves from experimentation to real workflow execution — but 44% of CRE investment committees still distrust AI-generated analysis, creating a gap operators can close.

items.headline: Landmark Properties Pivots to Senior Housing with Strategic New Hire
items.summary: Landmark Properties, known for its student housing portfolio, is entering senior housing through ground-up development and acquisitions. The company cited surging boomer-driven demand and severely limited new supply as key drivers. This institutional pivot signals growing conviction in senior housing as a top-tier asset class for the next decade.

items.headline: Senior Housing Demand Surges: 86% of Investors Plan to Add Exposure in 2026
items.summary: JLL survey data shows 86% of investors plan to increase senior housing allocations in 2026, driven by multi-year occupancy highs and constrained new supply. The U.S. population age 80+ is projected to grow 36.6% over the next decade versus just 5% total population growth. For Lloyd Jones, this demographic tailwind reinforces the long-term thesis for expanding the senior housing portfolio.

items.headline: Fed Holds at 3.50–3.75%; CRE Mortgage Rates Start at 5.39%
items.summary: The Federal Reserve left rates unchanged at its January 28 meeting and the 10-Year Treasury has stabilized around 4.00–4.25% since mid-2025. Commercial mortgage rates are now starting at 5.39% as of March 25, 2026, per Select Commercial. For senior housing operators and acquirers, this signals a more predictable debt environment heading into the active spring transaction season.

items.headline: Colliers Forecasts 15–20% CRE Transaction Volume Jump in 2026
items.summary: Colliers projects a significant rebound in CRE transaction volume as institutional and cross-border capital re-enters the market in 2026. The lending environment is described as healthy with abundant liquidity, though lenders remain selective — strong cash flow assets at conservative leverage are closing while marginal deals continue to stall. Senior housing assets with stabilized occupancy are well-positioned to transact.

items.headline: Proptech VC Surges 68% to $16.7B in 2025, AI-Native Platforms Lead
items.summary: Venture capital investment in proptech reached $16.7 billion in 2025, a 68% increase over 2024, driven by the pivot toward AI-native platforms over traditional software models. VCs are concentrating capital in the next generation of agentic AI tools rather than legacy SaaS. For CRE operators, this signals an accelerating pace of technology displacement — requiring strategic adoption decisions now, not next year.

items.headline: Agentic AI Is the Next Phase of Proptech — Automating CRE Workflows End-to-End
items.summary: ICSC reports that agentic AI is moving beyond content generation to autonomous decision-making and execution across retail and real estate operations. Operators are already deploying AI agents for procurement, pricing, and inventory management — reducing human oversight in repetitive workflows. Senior housing operators should evaluate where autonomous AI agents can replace back-office tasks and redeploy staff to resident-facing roles.

items.headline: 71% of Leaders Report Increased Stress; 40% Considering Leaving Their Roles
items.summary: A DDI Leadership Trends survey finds 71% of leaders are under significantly increased stress in 2026, with 40% actively considering leaving their positions. U.S. layoffs are at their highest point in 10 years, and restructured teams have increased individual leader workloads without reducing expectations. Retaining and investing in front-line and regional leadership talent is now a top strategic priority for senior housing operators.

items.headline: Senior Living Discounts Off 2019 Peaks Set to Vanish in 2026
items.summary: Assets have been priced at 30–50% discounts off 2019 peaks across price points and regions over the past 18 months. PE News reports this pricing window is closing rapidly as demand strengthens and institutional capital returns to the sector. Operators and investors with dry powder should anticipate a significantly more competitive acquisition environment by mid-2026.

items.headline: New Era Living CEO Plans Renovations and Expansion to Reinvigorate Brand
items.summary: New Era Living's incoming CEO is prioritizing capital improvements and portfolio expansion as core pillars of the 2026 growth strategy. This reflects a broader operator trend of investing in physical plant upgrades to compete for premium rate tiers and high-acuity residents. Communities with modernized amenities are commanding stronger occupancy and rate performance across the sector.

items.headline: CBRE: Cap Rates to Compress 5–15 bps for Quality Assets in 2026
items.summary: CBRE's 2026 Capital Markets Outlook projects modest cap rate compression of 5–15 basis points for most quality property types as confidence builds and capital flows return. Distressed and obsolete assets face rising default rates, widening the performance gap between core and non-core. Senior housing assets with strong operations and stabilized occupancy are well-positioned to benefit from this compression cycle.

items.headline: Real Agents Tell the Truth About AI: Opportunity, Not Replacement
items.summary: An Inman survey published March 26 found that real estate professionals view AI primarily as a time-saving tool for non-client-facing tasks rather than a replacement for human judgment in high-stakes decisions. Agents describe AI as one of the greatest opportunities for high-volume teams and solo operators to free up time. Senior housing sales and marketing teams can apply this directly to lead nurturing, tour follow-up, and content automation.

items.headline: 44% of CRE Investment Committees Distrust AI-Generated Analysis
items.summary: Commercial Observer reports that trust remains the primary barrier to deeper AI adoption in high-value CRE workflows, with nearly half of investment committees skeptical of AI-generated analysis. Firms building AI governance frameworks — including data validation and explainability standards — are seeing faster internal adoption rates. Operators who invest in transparent, auditable AI tools will gain a competitive edge over those still piloting siloed point solutions.

items.headline: HBR Study: LLMs Deliver Trendslop When Asked for Strategic Advice
items.summary: A March 2026 Harvard Business Review study found that large language models consistently produce generic, buzzword-heavy strategy recommendations — confident-sounding but lacking specificity or actionable insight. Researchers warn that leaders who mistake AI fluency for strategic depth are at risk of flawed decisions. The practical takeaway: use AI to accelerate research and drafting, but keep human judgment at the center of strategy.

items.headline: McKinsey: Three Decisions Every Organization Must Make Now in 2026
items.summary: McKinsey's State of Organizations 2026 research identifies three critical decisions: whether to restructure around AI capabilities, how to rebuild workforce trust after years of turbulence, and where to draw the line between human and automated decision-making. Organizations delaying these decisions risk talent loss and competitive erosion. The report emphasizes that speed of organizational learning — not strategy quality — is the defining differentiator this year.

items.headline: Gut Health Linked to 17% Reduction in Workplace Absenteeism
items.summary: New 2025–2026 research shows employees prioritizing high-fiber, gut-friendly diets report significantly fewer sick days, with some cohorts showing a 17% reduction in absenteeism. The link between gut microbiome health and cognitive performance is gaining traction with corporate wellness programs. Senior housing operators investing in staff wellness programs may find dietary interventions among the highest-ROI interventions available.

items.headline: AI-Personalized Wellness Goes Mainstream for Affluent Older Adults
items.summary: The Global Wellness Summit reports that 2026 marks a turning point for personalized wellness, with AI-driven analytics, at-home biomarker testing, and advanced wearables now accessible to mainstream consumers. Longevity protocols combining metabolic optimization, strength training, and community connection are being packaged as premium lifestyle offerings. Senior living communities that integrate these wellness modalities into programming will have a compelling differentiator for prospective residents and their families.

items.headline: Emotional Fitness Emerges as 2026's New Primary Wellness Category
items.summary: Global wellness experts are naming emotional fitness — the deliberate practice of cognitive resilience and emotional regulation — as a distinct new health category for 2026, separate from traditional mental health treatment. Corporate and senior living environments are among the fastest adopters of emotional fitness programming. For senior housing operators, programs targeting purpose, connection, and emotional resilience are increasingly key factors in family selection decisions.
